---
title: "yieldcurves"
subtitle: "Yield curve fitting and analysis in R"
author: "Charles Coverdale"
institute: "London, United Kingdom"
date: "2026-04-01"
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
format:
  beamer:
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 11pt
    navigation: empty
    theme: default
    colortheme: default
    fonttheme: default
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header: header.tex
    slide-level: 2
    keep-tex: false
    fig-align: center
    classoption:
      - t
---


## The question

\vspace{1.5em}

\begin{center}
\Large\bfseries\color{econblue}
How should we fit, decompose, and analyse the term structure\\[0.1em]
of interest rates in a single reproducible R interface?
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}

\begin{center}
\normalsize\itshape
Nineteen functions, one curve object, no heavy dependencies.
\end{center}

::: notes
Open with the question, not the package. The problem: yield-curve
work is routine but the R ecosystem delivers it piecemeal. We need
a single interface that matches the workflow of a rates analyst.
:::


## Why it matters

- **Central banks** publish fitted Nelson-Siegel and Svensson curves daily
- **Fixed-income portfolio managers** need duration, convexity, and key rate exposures
- **Relative-value traders** price carry and roll-down across the curve
- **Academic researchers** decompose curve movements via PCA and factor models

::: notes
Four distinct stakeholder groups, four live use cases. Point at
portfolio managers and central banks: those are the largest demand
segments. 60 seconds.
:::



## What is already out there

- **`YieldCurve`**: Nelson-Siegel and Svensson fits only; requires xts/zoo; no duration, convexity, PCA, or carry\footnote{\scriptsize Guirreri (2022), \textit{YieldCurve: Modelling and Estimation of the Yield Curve}, R package v5.2.}
- **`termstrc`**: broader coverage but archived from CRAN in 2018; no current maintenance
- **`fBonds`**: bond-pricing primitives only; no curve estimation
- **Bespoke scripts**: central-bank desks reimplement the same pipeline in-house

\vspace{0.5em}

The gap: **no single package delivers fitting, risk measures, and decomposition in one interface**.

::: notes
Respect existing packages. termstrc covered similar ground but is
archived. yieldcurves fills the vacuum with a unified curve object.
45 seconds.
:::



## What yieldcurves offers

1. **Coverage**: 19 functions spanning fitting (Nelson-Siegel, Svensson, cubic spline), extraction (forwards, discount factors, par-zero), risk (duration, convexity, Z-spread, KRD), and decomposition (PCA, level-slope-curvature, carry)
2. **Interface**: one `yc_curve` S3 object flows through every downstream function; inputs are plain numeric vectors
3. **Provenance**: pure R, no compiled code, no heavy dependencies, 172 unit tests

\vspace{0.5em}

On CRAN since March 2026. Parametric fits follow Nelson and Siegel (1987) and Svensson (1994)\footnote{\scriptsize Nelson \& Siegel (1987), \textit{Parsimonious modeling of yield curves}, J.\ Business 60(4); Svensson (1994), \textit{Estimating and interpreting forward interest rates}, IMF WP 94/114.}.

::: notes
Three bullets, three selling points. Emphasise the single curve
object: that is the usability advantage. CRAN presence signals
maturity. 45 seconds.
:::



## Fitting: Nelson-Siegel and Svensson

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig1_ns_svensson.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Parametric fits to a US Treasury curve.}
```

::: notes
First family. Nelson-Siegel three-factor fit against the four-factor
Svensson extension, both wrapped in the same \texttt{yc\_curve} object.
Point at the short-end fit quality. 25 seconds.
:::


## Decomposition: PCA level, slope, curvature

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig3_pca.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape PCA of monthly US Treasury curve changes.}
```

::: notes
Second family. Classical Litterman-Scheinkman factor pattern.
PC1 flat (level), PC2 monotone (slope), PC3 hump (curvature).
25 seconds.
:::


## Carry and roll-down

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig5_carry.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Carry and roll-down at a three-month horizon.}
```

::: notes
Third family. Relative-value trader view: where does holding a bond
to the next quarter earn its running yield versus its roll-down the
curve? Point at the belly. 25 seconds.
:::


## Risk: duration and convexity

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.65\textheight]{figures/fig4_duration.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Modified duration across maturities, zero-coupon versus par-coupon.}
```

::: notes
Fourth family. Risk measures dispatching on the same \texttt{yc\_curve}
object. Point at the wedge between zero and coupon duration at longer
maturities: that is the coupon-reinvestment effect. 25 seconds.
:::


## Central formulas

\vspace{0.3em}

\textbf{Nelson-Siegel} (three factors plus decay $\tau$):
\begin{equation}
y(m) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\!\left(\tfrac{1 - e^{-m/\tau}}{m/\tau}\right) + \beta_2\!\left(\tfrac{1 - e^{-m/\tau}}{m/\tau} - e^{-m/\tau}\right)
\end{equation}

\textbf{Svensson extension} (adds second curvature $\beta_3, \tau_2$):
\begin{equation}
y(m) = y_{\mathrm{NS}}(m) + \beta_3\!\left(\tfrac{1 - e^{-m/\tau_2}}{m/\tau_2} - e^{-m/\tau_2}\right)
\end{equation}

\textbf{Modified duration} (price sensitivity to parallel shift):
\begin{equation}
D_{\mathrm{mod}} = \frac{1}{P}\sum_{t} \frac{t \cdot CF_t}{(1 + y/k)^{kt + 1}}
\end{equation}

::: notes
Three equations, one family each. Do not read them aloud. Point at
the Nelson-Siegel curvature loading and the duration denominator.
The pattern: parsimonious functional forms applied consistently
across maturities. 60 seconds.
:::



## Package at a glance

::: columns
::: {.column width="58%"}

Function families:

- **Fitting**: `yc_nelson_siegel`, `yc_svensson`, `yc_cubic_spline`, `yc_fit`
- **Extraction**: `yc_predict`, `yc_forward`, `yc_discount`, `yc_par_to_zero`, `yc_zero_to_par`
- **Risk**: `yc_duration`, `yc_bond_duration`, `yc_zspread`, `yc_key_rate_duration`
- **Decomposition**: `yc_pca`, `yc_level_slope_curvature`, `yc_slope`, `yc_carry`

Deps: `cli`, `graphics`, `stats`. R $\geq$ 4.1.0.

:::
::: {.column width="40%"}

\begin{block}{One curve object}
Every fit returns a \texttt{yc\_curve}:

\texttt{maturities} | \texttt{rates} | \texttt{params} | \texttt{method}

All downstream functions dispatch on this class.
\end{block}

:::
:::

::: notes
Two columns. Left lists the families. Right highlights the shared
curve object in the blue block. The consistency is the pitch. 45
seconds.
:::



## Minimal working example

```r
library(yieldcurves)

# Fit a Nelson-Siegel curve to the current US Treasury quotes
mats  <- c(0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 30)
rates <- c(0.0495, 0.048, 0.046, 0.042, 0.041, 0.045, 0.050)
fit   <- yc_nelson_siegel(mats, rates)

# Derive forwards and three-month carry from the fitted curve
yc_forward(fit, maturities = c(2, 5, 10))
yc_carry(fit,   maturities = c(2, 5, 10), horizon = 0.25)
```

\footnotesize
All three downstream calls dispatch on the `yc_curve` class, so the
same idiom works whether you fit with Nelson-Siegel, Svensson, or a
cubic spline.

::: notes
Live-read the code. One fit, two downstream queries, zero reshaping.
That is the entire user experience. No xts or zoo required. 60 seconds.
:::



## US Treasury curve through the pandemic cycle

**Data.** FRED Constant Maturity Treasury rates at ten maturities, monthly from January 2019 to the most recent trading day. Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St.\ Louis.

**Question.** *How has the US Treasury term structure evolved across the pandemic monetary cycle?*

**Why this case.** Flagship global benchmark curve. Three distinct regimes in a five-year window: pre-pandemic flat-and-low, October 2023 peak inversion, current re-steepening. Reproducible with three public function calls from the `yc_curve` interface\footnote{\scriptsize Bauer \& Rudebusch (2020), \textit{Interest rates under falling stars}, American Economic Review 110(5).}.

::: notes
Setup only. No results on this slide. Point forward: hero figure is
next. Name the source, the period, the question, and why the US
curve is the right case. 45 seconds.
:::



## US Treasury curve: three regimes in five years

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\includegraphics[height=0.68\textheight]{figures/hero_figure.pdf}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.4em}

{\footnotesize\itshape US Treasury curves on three dates: pre-pandemic (Dec 2019), rate peak (Oct 2023), and most recent. Points are FRED CMT rates; lines are Nelson-Siegel fits.}
```

::: notes
The money slide. Ninety seconds. Walk the audience through each
curve: 2019 is flat and low, 2023 is inverted, the current shape is
positively-sloped again. The pattern: same fitting function, three
different stories about the macro cycle. Pause before moving on.
:::



## What yieldcurves does not yet do

- **Single-date fitting only**: no dynamic term-structure models, no affine Gaussian class
- **No return forecasting**: Cochrane-Piazzesi and similar predictive regressions out of scope
- **Bootstrap stripping**: annual and semi-annual coupons only; exotic schedules need preprocessing
- **No real-time central-bank feed**: users ingest Gurkaynak-Sack-Wright curves separately if desired

\vspace{0.5em}

**v0.2.0 roadmap**: arbitrage-free Nelson-Siegel following Christensen et al.\ (2011), Adrian-Crump-Moench term-premium regression, Cochrane-Piazzesi forecasting helpers, asset-swap spread computation.

::: notes
Honest limitations. Name each one. Frame v0.2.0 as a commitment, not
a vague plan. The arbitrage-free extension matters most to academic
users. 45 seconds.
:::



## Contact, code, paper

::: columns
::: {.column width="62%"}

```{=latex}
{\large\bfseries Charles Coverdale\par}
\vspace{0.5em}
\texttt{charles.f.coverdale@gmail.com}\\
\texttt{github.com/charlescoverdale/yieldcurves}\\
\texttt{charlescoverdale.github.io/publications}
\vspace{0.9em}

\textbf{Acknowledgements.} Data: Federal Reserve Bank of St.\ Louis (FRED, public domain). Thanks to the CRAN maintainers, \texttt{cli} developers, and reviewers of \emph{yieldcurves} v0.1.0.
```

:::
::: {.column width="35%"}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/qrcode_paper.png}

\vspace{0.3em}

{\footnotesize\itshape Scan for paper PDF}
\end{center}

:::
:::

::: notes
Close cleanly. Name, email, GitHub, publications. Point at the QR
code. Acknowledge FRED and CRAN. Open for questions.
:::
